retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The first person to encounter the patient is the triage nurse, who must be familiar with the symptoms of the disorder. Time is of the essence, and the nurse should be aware of torsion, quickly admit the patient, and quickly notify the ED physician. The ED physician should consult with a radiologist for the appropriate test and, at the same time, consult with the urologist. The nurses should prepare the patient as if he will be going for surgery by keeping the child NPO and having all the blood work completed.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the epidemiology of testicular torsion. Outline the presenting signs and symptoms of testicular torsion. Summarize considerations that influence the management of testicular torsion. Explain how the facilitation of interprofessional team education and discussion can optimize the effective detection of testicular torsion and inform the need for subsequent evaluations. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In neonates, bilateral scrotal exploration is done. Contralateral orchiopexy is always done to prevent future torsion. Patients who require an orchiectomy for a non-viable testis usually have a testicular prosthesis inserted. The prosthesis is usually inserted 4-6 months after the initial surgery to allow for the inflammation to subside

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound is not a perfect test for testicular torsion, especially in the very young. For example, 40% of neonatal testicles may have no apparent color flow Doppler. If the clinical concern is high, seek urologic surgery consultation immediately. Any delay in treatment could result in testicular necrosis and loss. The typical window of opportunity for surgical intervention and testicular salvage is 6 hours from the onset of pain. Therefore, early urologic surgery consultation upon presentation may be critical even in the absence of confirmatory testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Orchiopexy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The indications for orchiopexy include: Failure of the testis to spontaneously descend into scrotum by 6 months [1] [23] Acute or intermittent testicular torsion

retrieved_docs.title: Ultrasound of the Urinary Tract -- Indications -- Testicular Pain and Scrotal Masses
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal scrotal sac (hydrocele or spermatocele) Abnormal scrotal wall lesion Unexplained retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy History of testicular cancer, leukemia, or lymphoma Infertility Possible testicular torsion Scrotal abscess Scrotal mass identified by other imaging Scrotal swelling Scrotal or testicular mass on examination Scrotal surgery follow-up Testicular nodule or lump Testicular pain (chronic and acute) Testicular trauma Varicocele

retrieved_docs.content: Educate patient on surgery for testicular torsion Educate patient on management of acute epididymitis Hand washing and prevention of infection Safe sex practices

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptorchidism -- Treatment / Management -- Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: According to the AUA guidelines, surgery is advised for congenital undescended testes between the ages of 6 and 18 months. [8] Many specialists endorse early surgical intervention, often around 6 months, to optimize testicular growth and preserve fertility. Furthermore, performing the procedure at a younger age is preferable because the distance the testes must be moved increases as the child ages.

retrieved_docs.title: Orchiopexy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cryptorchidism is the most common congenital defect of the male genitalia, occurring in about 3% of full-term male births. Spontaneous resolution and testicular descent will occur in 80% of neonates by the age of three months. For testicles that remain undescended at 6 months of age or later, surgery is recommended. Diagnostic methods and imaging are reviewed, as well as the management of bilateral impalpable testes, which present a unique diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. Recent developments include laparoscopic orchiopexies, the use of internal abdominal testicular fixation techniques, and new recommendations regarding the optimal timing of corrective surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Scrotum Pain -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Other imaging modalities are also available. Radionuclide scrotal imaging (RNSI) was at one point the primary modality since its implementation in the 1970s. In the differentiation of testicular torsion and epididymo-orchitis, there is a reported sensitivity range of 89% to 98% and specificity of 90% to 100%. However, this modality is prone to false positives particularly in cases of spermatocele, hydrocele, and inguinal hernias, and may lead to unnecessary surgical exploration when compared to modern ultrasonography. MRI had 93% sensitivity and 100% specificity for diagnosing torsion; however, availability and length of time required to complete may limit its appropriateness. CT imaging is useful when there is a concern for necrotizing skin and soft tissue infections and Fournier gangrene. [11] [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Orchiopexy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Participants in this activity gain insights into the indications and techniques of orchiopexy, including recent advancements such as laparoscopic procedures and internal abdominal testicular fixation techniques. To optimize care for infants and children with cryptorchidism, collaboration with an interprofessional team comprising pediatricians, pediatric surgeons or urologists, caregivers, and patients is emphasized. Through interdisciplinary collaboration, healthcare professionals enhance their ability to provide comprehensive care, ensuring timely diagnosis, appropriate intervention, and long-term management.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Scrotal complaints are relatively common both in primary care and in the emergency department and comprise at least 0.5 percent of all emergency department visits. Testicular torsion is a true urologic emergency, and early identification is critical to prevent the need for testicular amputation. Ultrasound is the ideal imaging modality to evaluate the scrotal contents. This activity will review the most common causes of testicular torsion, the common presenting signs and symptoms, and the treatment approach according to current evidence. This activity will highlight the role of the interprofessional team in recognizing and treating testicular torsion.

retrieved_docs.title: Cryptorchidism -- Evaluation -- Bilateral Cryptorchidism
retrieved_docs.content: Bilateral cryptorchidism with palpable testes can be addressed through surgery, although there is some debate regarding the optimal timing for this intervention. Some experts advocate for 2 separate procedures, allowing complete healing of 1 testicle before addressing the contralateral side. Although this approach ensures the survival of at least 1 testicle, it requires subjecting a young child to 2 separate procedures and anesthesia. Consequently, many specialists prefer to repair both sides in a single surgery, primarily due to the overall low complication rate of about 1%. [12] Infertility may be observed in 35% to 75% of men who initially presented with bilateral cryptorchidism during childhood.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency that almost always presents to the emergency department. The disorder is usually managed by an interprofessional team.

retrieved_docs.title: Testicular Torsion -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Testis tumor Epididymitis Hydrocele Traumatic hematoma Orchitis

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Scrotum Pain (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: The definitive treatment of testicular torsion is surgical exploration and detorsion, usually followed by orchiopexy to prevent recurrent torsion. Manual detorsion can and often should be attempted at the bedside although its success rate is widely variable and has been reported to range from 25% to 80%. In patients with testicular torsion, the affected testicle is rotated medially. Therefore, manual detorsion should be attempted first from a medial-to-lateral direction (“opening of the book”) and may require 180 to 720 more degrees. Specifically, this requires a counterclockwise rotation of the patient's right testicle or a clockwise rotation of the patient's left testicle, similar to the action of opening the spine of a book. Manual detorsion is contraindicated if pain and/or suspected torsion have been present for longer than 6 hours. Point-of-care ultrasound may be usefully in evaluating the direction and effectiveness of this maneuver. Successful manual detorsion should result in near immediate relief of pain. Surgical exploration is still mandated even in the event manual detorsion is successful.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1665
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopy (gi only) serves as a broad minimally invasive surgical platform that encompasses several specific gynecologic procedures. Laparoscopic lysis of adhesions is identified as a child procedure within this platform, indicating it is a specialized application of laparoscopy (gi only). Both laparoscopic salpingostomy and laparoscopy with removal of adnexal structures are classified as types of laparoscopy (gi only), confirming that they share the same operative approach. Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral is also mapped to laparoscopy (gi only), further illustrating that bilateral removal of portions of the fallopian tubes is performed using this gastrointestinal‑focused laparoscopic technique. In clinical practice, the use of laparoscopy (gi only) frequently co‑occurs with conditions such as ovarian torsion (التواء المبيض), tubo‑ovarian abscess (خراج بوقي مبيضي), and tubal rupture (فَغْرُ البُوْق). These associations suggest that such pathologies are common indications for, or complications of, the laparoscopic procedures listed, highlighting the relevance of laparoscopy (gi only) in the diagnosis and management of acute gynecologic emergencies.

retrieved_docs.content: In obstetric practice, the overarching topic “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” includes the specific condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which is classified as a subcategory (has parent) within these care indications. This relationship highlights that malposition and malpresentation are considered part of the broader set of indications guiding clinical management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1779
retrieved_docs.content: استئصال قناة فالوب (salpingectomy) is categorized under Female Reproductive System Procedures and functions as a parent entity for several specific laparoscopic techniques. The procedures Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral; Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, unilateral; Laparoscopic salpingectomy, bilateral; and Laparoscopic salpingectomy, unilateral are all child procedures of استئصال قناة فالوب, representing bilateral or unilateral removal of the fallopian tube, either partially or completely, using minimally invasive laparoscopy. Clinically, استئصال قناة فالوب frequently co‑occurs with مرض التهاب الحوض (pelvic inflammatory disease), reflecting its common use in the management of this infection and its complications. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship of these procedures within female reproductive system interventions and their association with مرض التهاب الحوض helps clinicians select appropriate surgical approaches and anticipate related clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2382
retrieved_docs.content: Antenatal/postnatal care encompasses the management of severe birth‑related injuries, recognizing that a Mutilerende ingreep voor bevalling (a potentially mutilating procedure during delivery) may occur and that prompt Repair of obstetric laceration is an essential component of comprehensive maternal care. By addressing both the prevention of traumatic delivery interventions and the timely surgical repair of obstetric lacerations, antenatal/postnatal care aims to protect maternal health and promote optimal recovery after childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 544
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopy (gi only) is the central minimally invasive surgical modality for a variety of gynecologic procedures. Specific operations that are classified as direct descendants or instances of this laparoscopic approach include Laparoscopic lysis of adhesions, Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral, Laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, Laparoscopic salpingostomy, Laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, and Laparoscopy with removal of adnexal structures. These procedures are integral to the management of ectopic pregnancy; they fall under the hierarchical category Procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy, which itself is a subcategory of removal of ectopic pregnancy. Within this framework, Fetotoxic injection of ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy and Laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy are identified as child procedures of the ectopic‑pregnancy management group, while Laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy is directly mapped as a primary procedure for removal of ectopic pregnancy. Clinically, laparoscopy (gi only) frequently co‑occurs with conditions such as التواء المبيض (ovarian torsion), خراج بوقي مبيضي (tubo‑ovarian abscess), and فَغْرُ البُوْق (tubal rupture), indicating that these acute gynecologic emergencies often necessitate laparoscopic intervention. Recognizing the hierarchical relationships among these laparoscopic techniques and their association with ectopic pregnancy management and related gynecologic pathologies supports comprehensive patient care and informed surgical planning.

These findings reflect عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين (instability of fetal position) and are used in clinical guidelines for “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” to guide recognition, classification, and management, which may include specialized labor management, operative delivery, cesarean section, or intensified monitoring to protect maternal and neonatal safety. Cephalopelvic disproportion (عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض) is a child concept of Fetal Positions and Presentations and is further connected to structural fetal anomalies such as fetal myelomeningocele and fetal brain hydrocephalus (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب) that cause the mismatch. The fetal period is also associated with pathological states including foetale ascites (wanverhouding; foetale ascites), foetale hydrops (wanverhouding; foetale hydrops), and other fetal period pathologies that can contribute to abnormal presentations.
